concepts.title: Section 7.1: Shell Types and Basic Concepts

content.why_exists: Shell scripting automates repetitive tasks and complex workflows.
content.real_world_problem: A system administrator needs to perform the same backup, log rotation, and cleanup tasks every night at 2 AM. Instead of staying awake to run commands manually, they create a script that automates the entire process and can be scheduled to run unattended.
content.daily_life_analogy: Shell scripting is like **writing a recipe**. Instead of explaining each step verbally every time you cook, you write down the ingredients and instructions once. Anyone (or any computer) can follow the recipe to get consistent results.
content.problem: You need to perform the same sequence of commands repeatedly.
content.beginner_explanation: A shell script is a file containing shell commands that can be executed as a program.

shell_types.bash: Bourne Again SHell - most common, feature-rich
shell_types.zsh: Z Shell - advanced, compatible with bash

content.script_creation: Create file with .sh extension, make it executable with chmod +x
content.definition: Shell scripts are programs written in shell command language.

content.why_exists: Tell the system which interpreter to use for the script.
content.real_world_problem: A developer creates a Python script but the system tries to run it with the wrong interpreter (bash instead of python). The script fails with syntax errors because bash doesn't understand Python code. The shebang ensures the correct interpreter is used.
content.daily_life_analogy: The shebang is like **the language label on a document**. When someone finds a document written in Spanish, the label tells them to find a Spanish speaker to read it. Without the label, they might try reading it in English and misunderstand everything.
content.problem: Script fails because system doesn't know which shell to use.
content.definition: Shebang specifies the interpreter for executing the script.

content.real_world_problem: A critical backup script fails during an emergency restore. The new administrator can't understand what the script does or how to fix it because there are no comments explaining the logic. Hours are wasted deciphering uncommented code under pressure.
content.daily_life_analogy: Comments are like **directions in a recipe**. Without them, you know the ingredients (commands) but not why you add salt before vs after cooking, or what happens if you skip a step. Good comments explain the 'why' and 'what if' scenarios.
content.problem: Complex script is hard to understand and modify.
content.beginner_explanation: # starts a comment. Everything after # is ignored.
content.best_practices: Comment what and why, not how. Use header comments for script purpose.
content.definition: Comments explain code purpose and are ignored by interpreter.

content.why_exists: Store and manipulate data in scripts.
content.real_world_problem: A deployment script needs to work across multiple environments (development, staging, production) with different database servers, file paths, and credentials. Hard-coded values would require separate scripts for each environment, leading to maintenance nightmares.
content.daily_life_analogy: Variables are like **labeled containers in a kitchen**. Instead of hard-coding '2 cups flour' in every recipe, you use a variable 'flour_amount = 2 cups'. Now you can reuse the recipe for cookies (2 cups) or bread (4 cups) by just changing the variable.
content.beginner_explanation: Variables store values. No spaces around = in assignment.
content.definition: Variables store data that can be referenced and modified.

content.why_exists: Execute different code based on conditions.
content.problem: Script needs to behave differently based on circumstances.
content.beginner_explanation: If condition is true, execute then block. Optionally execute else block.
examples: if [ -f file.txt ]; then echo "File exists"; fi
if [ "$var" == "value" ]; then echo "Equal"; else echo "Not equal"; fi
if [ $num -gt 10 ]; then echo "Big"; elif [ $num -gt 5 ]; then echo "Medium"; else echo "Small"; fi
content.definition: Conditional statements execute code based on true/false conditions.

commands.command: if [ condition ]; then commands; else commands; fi
if [ condition ]; then commands; elif [ condition ]; then commands; fi

concepts.title: Section 7.6: Loops - For, While, and Until

content.real_world_problem: A system administrator needs to check disk usage on 50 servers, restart services on 20 web servers, or process log files from multiple applications. Writing individual commands for each would be impractical and error-prone.
content.daily_life_analogy: Loops are like **assembly line production**. Instead of building one car at a time with individual instructions, you create a loop that repeats the same process for each car on the line. For loops handle known quantities, while/until loops handle unknown wait conditions.
content.problem: Need to process multiple items or repeat until condition met.
content.beginner_explanation: For: iterate over list. While: loop while condition true. Until: loop until condition true.

loop_types.for: for item in list; do commands; done

examples: for file in *.txt; do echo "Processing $file"; done
until [ -f ready.txt ]; do sleep 1; done; echo "File is ready"
content.control_statements: break (exit loop), continue (skip to next iteration)

commands.command: for var in list; do commands; done

content.why_exists: Organize code into reusable, modular blocks.
content.real_world_problem: A deployment script needs to validate configuration, backup data, update software, and restart services. Without functions, this logic would be repeated across multiple scripts, making maintenance difficult and introducing bugs when changes are needed.
content.daily_life_analogy: Functions are like **kitchen appliances**. Instead of explaining how to chop vegetables every time you write a recipe, you have a 'chop()' function. Recipes become shorter and focus on the high-level process, while the detailed chopping steps are encapsulated in the appliance.
content.problem: Same code repeated multiple times in script.
content.beginner_explanation: Functions group commands that can be called multiple times.
content.parameters: Functions can access $1, $2, etc. for arguments.
content.definition: Functions are named blocks of code that can be called repeatedly.

concepts.title: Section 7.8: Pipes and Redirection in Scripts

content.real_world_problem: A log analysis script needs to process error messages from multiple sources, filter out noise, save results to a report file, and also log any script errors separately. Without proper redirection, errors would mix with data and debugging would be impossible.
content.daily_life_analogy: Pipes and redirection are like **plumbing in a house**. You redirect water (data) from the sink (command output) to the dishwasher (another command) via pipes, or redirect it to the garden hose (file) for storage. Leaks (unhandled errors) flood the kitchen.
content.problem: Script needs to read from files, write to files, or chain commands.
examples:
sort << EOF
banana
apple
cherry
EOF
content.definition: Redirection controls where input comes from and output goes.

content.real_world_problem: A deployment script needs to extract version numbers from filenames, remove file extensions, convert Windows paths to Unix paths, and generate database connection strings from configuration templates. Raw strings need transformation before use.
content.daily_life_analogy: String operations are like **editing a manuscript**. You cut out sections (substrings), replace words (search/replace), trim margins (remove prefixes/suffixes), and count words (length). Just as an editor shapes raw text into a polished article, these operations shape data for processing.
content.problem: Need to extract parts of strings or modify text.
content.beginner_explanation: Bash provides parameter expansion for string operations.
content.definition: String operations manipulate text using parameter expansion.

content.why_exists: Capture output of commands for use in scripts.
content.real_world_problem: A monitoring script needs to check disk usage and send an alert if it exceeds 90%. It must capture the output of 'df' command, extract the percentage, compare it to the threshold, and take action - all in a single automated workflow.
content.daily_life_analogy: Command substitution is like **asking a calculator for an answer and writing it down**. Instead of showing '2+2' on the page, you write the result '4'. The script captures the 'answer' from commands and uses it in subsequent calculations.
content.problem: Need to use result of one command as input to another.
content.beginner_explanation: Execute command and substitute its output.
content.definition: Command substitution captures command output as a string.

content.why_exists: Handle errors gracefully and debug scripts.
content.real_world_problem: A critical backup script fails midway through a 100GB transfer because a network drive becomes unavailable. Without proper error handling, the script exits silently, leaving the administrator unaware that the backup is incomplete and the data is at risk.
content.daily_life_analogy: Error handling is like **driving with safety features**. Set -e is the automatic brake (stop on first problem), traps are airbags (emergency cleanup), and debug mode is the dashboard display (showing exactly what's happening). Good error handling prevents disasters.
content.problem: Script fails silently or gives cryptic errors.
content.beginner_explanation: Check exit codes, use traps, enable debugging.

error_handling.set_options: set -e (exit on error), set -u (undefined variables), set -x (debug)

examples: if ! command; then echo "Command failed"; exit 1; fi
content.definition: Error handling ensures scripts fail gracefully and provide useful feedback.

concepts.title: Section 7.12: Exit Codes and Script Termination

content.why_exists: Communicate script success/failure to calling programs.
content.real_world_problem: A deployment pipeline runs multiple scripts in sequence. If the database migration script fails but doesn't signal failure properly, the application deployment continues and crashes in production. Proper exit codes prevent cascading failures.
content.daily_life_analogy: Exit codes are like **restaurant feedback cards**. A successful meal (exit 0) means 'come back anytime', while food poisoning (exit 1) means 'never return'. The calling program reads the feedback and decides the next action based on the experience.
content.problem: Calling script doesn't know if it succeeded.
examples: command || exit 1  # Exit if command fails
content.definition: Exit codes indicate script execution result to parent process.

content.why_exists: Automate complex workflows and system administration tasks.
content.real_world_problem: A company needs to deploy application updates to 500 servers across multiple data centers, including pre-flight checks, rolling updates, health validation, and rollback procedures. Manual execution would take weeks and guarantee human errors.
content.daily_life_analogy: Automation scripts are like **industrial robots on an assembly line**. They combine simple tools (variables, loops, conditionals) into complex workflows that execute flawlessly 24/7. Just as a robot builds a car from thousands of parts, a script orchestrates servers from simple commands.
content.problem: Manual tasks are time-consuming and error-prone.
content.beginner_explanation: Combine all scripting concepts to create powerful automation tools.
content.best_practices: Use functions, validate inputs, log actions, handle errors
content.definition: Automation scripts combine shell commands to perform complex tasks automatically.
